questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">In <strong>Essay of Dramatic Poesy</strong> which character does Dryden himself represent ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Neander.

Key Points

  • John Dryden's "An Essay of Dramatic Poesy" (1668) is a seminal work of English literary criticism written in the form of a dialogue.
  • The character Neander (which means "new man") is widely recognized as the spokesperson for John Dryden himself.
  • In the essay, Neander defends the English dramatic tradition against the French and argues for the superiority of modern English plays, particularly those of Shakespeare and Beaumont and Fletcher.
  • Neander advocates for "liveliness" in drama and defends the use of rhyme in serious plays, positioning himself as a balanced voice between classical rules and creative freedom.
  • The dialogue takes place on a boat on the River Thames while the characters listen to the sounds of a naval battle between the English and the Dutch.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2.

Additional Information

  • Crites: Represents Sir Robert Howard. He defends the Ancients and argues that they reached a perfection in drama that modern writers should try to emulate.
  • Eugenius: Represents Charles Sackville, Lord Buckhurst. He favors the Moderns over the Ancients, arguing that modern writers have improved upon the foundations laid by the Greeks and Romans.
  • Lisideius: Represents Sir Charles Sedley. He argues that French drama is superior to English drama because it strictly adheres to the classical Three Unities (Time, Place, and Action) and avoids the "tumult" of the English stage.

questions.statement: Who has written the famous text ‘In Search of Our Mother’s Gardens’ ?
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Alice Walker.

Key Points

  • In Search of Our Mother's Gardens: Womanist Prose is a non-fiction collection of essays, reviews, and articles by Alice Walker, published in 1983.
  • The book is significant for introducing and defining the term "womanist" to the public consciousness.
  • Walker defines a womanist as a Black feminist or feminist of color who is "committed to survival and wholeness of entire people, male and female."
  • The title essay explores the history of African American women and how their creative spirits were suppressed during slavery and the post-slavery era.
  • Walker argues that even when denied the right to read or write, Black women expressed their artistic genius through everyday activities like gardening and quilting.
  • Alice Walker is also a Pulitzer Prize-winning novelist, most famous for her book The Color Purple.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Barbara Smith: A key figure in Black feminism and a co-founder of the Combahee River Collective and Kitchen Table: Women of Color Press. She played a major role in defining the intersectional nature of Black women's experiences.
  • Hester Eisenstein: A contemporary American sociologist and feminist theorist known for her work Contemporary Feminist Thought, which analyzes the evolution of feminist ideologies.
  • Judith Squires: A prominent British political theorist whose work focuses on gender and politics, specifically on gender mainstreaming and feminist theories of representation.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Coleridge speaks of how many types of imagination ?</span>
options: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">4 types (primary, secondary, tertiary and fancy)</span> /
<span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">3 types (primary, secondary and fancy)</span> /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is 3 types (primary, secondary and fancy).

Key Points

  • Samuel Taylor Coleridge detailed his theory of the human mind and creative faculties in his seminal critical work, "Biographia Literaria" (1817).
  • He categorizes the faculties involved in perception and artistic creation into three distinct types: Primary Imagination, Secondary Imagination, and Fancy.
  • Primary Imagination: He defines this as the "living Power and prime Agent of all human Perception." It is a universal, subconscious faculty that allows all humans to perceive and make sense of the world.
  • Secondary Imagination: This is an "echo" of the primary imagination, functioning consciously and voluntarily. It is the specific faculty of the poet or artist that "dissolves, diffuses, dissipates, in order to re-create."
  • Fancy: Coleridge distinguishes Fancy from the imagination, describing it as a lower, mechanical faculty. It is a "mode of memory" that merely rearranges existing perceptions and memories without transforming their nature.
  • By identifying these three, Coleridge aimed to explain the "synthetic and magical power" of poetry as an organic rather than mechanical process.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 3.

Additional Information

  • Biographia Literaria: This work is considered one of the most important texts in English literary criticism, bridging the gap between philosophy and literature.
  • Willing Suspension of Disbelief: This is another famous concept introduced by Coleridge in the same work, referring to the reader's decision to overlook the implausibility of a narrative for the sake of enjoyment.
  • Samuel Taylor Coleridge: Along with William Wordsworth, Coleridge was a founder of the Romantic Movement in England and a co-author of the influential "Lyrical Ballads."

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">&lsquo;Objective correlative&rsquo;, a term, introduced by T. S. Eliot, was coined by which American painter and poet ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Washington Allston.

Key Points

  • Washington Allston (1779–1843) was an influential American painter and poet, often called the "American Titian."
  • While the term "objective correlative" is most famously associated with the modernist critic T. S. Eliot, it was actually coined by Allston much earlier.
  • Allston first used the term in his Lectures on Art (published posthumously in 1850), specifically in the introductory chapter written around 1840.
  • In Allston's usage, the term referred to the way external objects or nature serve as a "correlative" to the internal human spirit or mental states.
  • T. S. Eliot later popularized the term in his 1919 essay "Hamlet and His Problems," defining it as "a set of objects, a situation, a chain of events which shall be the formula of that particular emotion."
  • The concept remains a cornerstone of literary criticism, particularly in the study of how emotions are evoked in poetry and drama.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • Paul H. Fry: A distinguished professor of English at Yale University, widely known for his lectures and publications on literary theory and the history of criticism.
  • M. H. Abrams: An American literary critic and author of the influential work "The Mirror and the Lamp," which categorizes theories of art; he was also the founding editor of the Norton Anthology of English Literature.
  • Sylvia Plath: A major 20th-century American poet and novelist, best known for her collection "Ariel" and the semi-autobiographical novel "The Bell Jar," and recognized as a leading figure in the confessional poetry movement.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The <strong>Madwoman in The Attic</strong> was a feminist text written by which of these writers ?</span>
options: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar</span> /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar.

Key Points

  • The Madwoman in the Attic: The Woman Writer and the Nineteenth-Century Literary Imagination is a landmark 1979 book by Sandra Gilbert and Susan Gubar.
  • The title is derived from Charlotte Brontë's Jane Eyre, specifically referring to Bertha Mason, Rochester's wife, who is kept locked in the attic.
  • The text is a foundational work in feminist literary criticism, examining the distinct tradition of female writing in the 19th century.
  • Gilbert and Gubar argue that 19th-century women writers were confined by a patriarchal literary tradition that reduced female characters to two archetypes: the "angel" or the "monster."
  • The authors explore how writers like Jane Austen, Mary Shelley, the Brontë sisters, George Eliot, and Emily Dickinson subverted these tropes to express their own creative struggles.
  • The work was a finalist for the Pulitzer Prize and remains a core text in women's studies and literary theory.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • Toril Moi: A prominent feminist critic known for her book Sexual/Textual Politics (1985), which critiques the Anglo-American feminist tradition represented by Gilbert and Gubar.
  • Elaine Showalter: A leading feminist critic who wrote A Literature of Their Own (1977) and coined the term "gynocriticism" to describe the study of women as writers.
  • Virginia Woolf: An iconic modernist novelist and essayist whose work A Room of One's Own (1929) laid the intellectual foundation for modern feminist literary criticism.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">What is the term called, often associated with Jean Baudrillard&#39;s notion of the &#39;reality effect&#39; ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Simulacra/Simulacrum.

Key Points

  • Jean Baudrillard was a French sociologist, philosopher, and cultural theorist whose work is fundamental to postmodernism and post-structuralism.
  • The term "Simulacrum" (plural: simulacra) refers to a copy without an original, or something that replaces reality with its representation.
  • Baudrillard developed this concept in his influential book "Simulacra and Simulation" (1981).
  • He argued that in contemporary society, signs and symbols have become more real than the reality they are supposed to represent, a condition he termed "hyperreality."
  • The "reality effect" in this context describes how simulations create a sense of reality that obscures the fact that the original reality no longer exists or never did.
  • His theories explore how media, consumerism, and technology shape our perception of the world and dissolve the boundaries between the real and the imaginary.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2.

Additional Information

  • Singularity: Often associated with the technological singularity, a hypothetical future point where technological growth becomes uncontrollable, or used in mathematics and physics to describe a point where a function takes an infinite value.
  • Homophobia: A term used to describe a range of negative attitudes, feelings, or actions toward people who are identified or perceived as being non-heterosexual.
  • Interpellation: A concept introduced by the Marxist philosopher Louis Althusser to describe the process by which ideology constitutes the individual as a subject.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Who has originally coined the term &#39;Intertextuality&#39; ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Julia Kristeva.

Key Points

  • Julia Kristeva, a Bulgarian-French philosopher, literary critic, and psychoanalyst, coined the term "intertextuality" in 1966.
  • She introduced the concept in her essays, most notably in "Word, Dialogue and Novel," where she synthesized Ferdinand de Saussure’s semiotics with Mikhail Bakhtin’s dialogism.
  • Kristeva defined intertextuality as the idea that any text is constructed as a "mosaic of quotations" and that any text is the absorption and transformation of another.
  • The concept suggests that a text's meaning is not fixed but is shaped by its relationship to other texts, making the reader an active participant in creating meaning.
  • Her work became a foundational element of post-structuralist literary theory.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2.

Additional Information

  • Bloom (Harold Bloom): An American literary critic known for his theory of "The Anxiety of Influence," which explores how poets struggle against the influence of their precursors.
  • Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak: A prominent post-colonial theorist and scholar known for her influential essay "Can the Subaltern Speak?" and her translation of Jacques Derrida’s "Of Grammatology."
  • Louis Gates (Henry Louis Gates Jr.): An American literary critic and historian who developed the theory of "Signifyin(g)" in African-American literary tradition in his book "The Signifying Monkey."

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">In the words of Homi K. Bhabha, what is the name for the strategic reversal of the process of domination through disavowal ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Hybridity.

Key Points

  • Homi K. Bhabha is a critical figure in post-colonial studies, known for developing concepts such as mimicry, third space, and hybridity.
  • In his influential work The Location of Culture (1994), specifically in the essay "Signs Taken for Wonders," Bhabha defines hybridity as the "strategic reversal of the process of domination through disavowal."
  • Hybridity describes the way in which colonial authority is undermined when it is translated into the colonial environment, creating a "third space" that is neither the colonizer's nor the colonized's original culture.
  • It represents a displacement of value where the colonized subject adopts elements of the dominant culture but transforms them, thereby challenging the purity and authority of the colonial power.
  • Bhabha argues that hybridity is a site of resistance because it prevents the colonial identity from being a fixed, stable entity.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 3.

Additional Information

  • Hypertext: A term coined by Ted Nelson, referring to a software system that links topics on a screen to related information and graphics, typically accessed by a point-and-click method.
  • Heuristic: A technique or method of problem-solving, learning, or discovery that employs a practical approach, not guaranteed to be perfect, but sufficient for immediate goals.
  • Habitus: A concept developed by the French sociologist Pierre Bourdieu, referring to the ingrained habits, skills, and dispositions that individuals possess due to their life experiences and social environment.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The idea that &#39;the phono/lexico/grammatical constituents of a literary work and the differences between linguistic signs create meaning&#39; is most closely associated with which theory ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Structuralism.

Key Points

  • Structuralism is a literary and cultural theory that emerged in the mid-20th century, drawing heavily from the linguistic theories of Ferdinand de Saussure.
  • It operates on the principle that meaning is not inherent in individual words or things but is generated through the relationships and differences between linguistic signs within a structured system.
  • Saussure argued that a "sign" consists of a signifier (the sound or written form) and a signified (the concept), and that the connection between them is arbitrary.
  • The focus on "phono/lexico/grammatical constituents" reflects the structuralist interest in the underlying rules (langue) that govern how language and meaning are constructed.
  • Structuralist critics analyze texts to uncover the deep-seated systems or "structures" (such as binary oppositions like light/dark or good/evil) that allow a work to convey meaning.
  • Meaning is thus seen as a product of the internal system of language rather than a reflection of external reality or the author's personal intent.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • New Criticism: A school of formalist literary criticism that advocates for "close reading" of the text as an independent entity, focusing on internal contradictions, irony, and paradox rather than linguistic systems of difference.
  • Postcolonialism: A theoretical approach that examines the cultural, political, and economic legacies of colonialism and imperialism, focusing on issues of identity, power, and resistance.
  • Objectification: In social and literary theory, this refers to the process of treating a person as a mere object or commodity, often explored in feminist theory regarding the portrayal of women.

questions.statement: Which French structuralist, following Derrida, made a useful distinction between 'work' and 'text' ?
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Roland Barthes.

Key Points

  • Roland Barthes was a French literary theorist, philosopher, and semiotician whose work transitioned from structuralism to post-structuralism.
  • He made the famous distinction between 'work' and 'text' in his influential essay "From Work to Text" (1971).
  • According to Barthes, a 'work' is a physical object, a finished product that can be held in the hand and is often associated with a fixed, singular meaning intended by the author.
  • In contrast, a 'text' is a methodological field, a process of language and meaning-making that is active, plural, and open to endless interpretations.
  • While a work is seen as stable and consumed by the reader, a text is experienced through the reader's active engagement and production of meaning.
  • This distinction marked a shift in literary criticism from focusing on the author's authority to focusing on the play of language and the role of the reader.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Wittgenstein: Ludwig Wittgenstein was an Austrian-British philosopher who worked primarily in logic and the philosophy of language, known for his concepts of "language games" and "private language."
  • Lacan: Jacques Lacan was a French psychoanalyst who combined structural linguistics with Freudian theory, focusing on the role of the "Symbolic," "Imaginary," and "Real" in the human psyche.
  • Freud: Sigmund Freud was the Austrian founder of psychoanalysis; his theories on the unconscious and the Oedipus complex laid the groundwork for many structuralist and post-structuralist critiques.

questions.statement: According to Michael Holquist, what is the term used to describe the way of conceiving the world as made up of a rolling mass of languages, each of which has its own formal markers ?
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Heteroglossia.

Key Points

  • Michael Holquist, a renowned scholar and translator of Mikhail Bakhtin, defines Heteroglossia as the base condition governing the operation of meaning in any utterance.
  • In his introduction and glossary to Bakhtin's "The Dialogic Imagination," Holquist describes it as a way of conceiving the world as made up of a rolling mass of languages, each of which has its own formal markers.
  • The term refers to the coexistence of and conflict between different types of speech or "socio-ideological" languages within a single national language (such as professional jargons, age groups, or social classes).
  • According to this view, no language is a monolithic system; instead, it is a site of struggle between centripetal (unifying) and centrifugal (stratifying) forces.
  • This concept is central to Bakhtin's theory of the novel, which he sees as the genre best suited to represent the diversity of human speech.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Genealogy: A term most famously used by Friedrich Nietzsche and later Michel Foucault to describe a historical technique in which one questions the commonly understood emergence of various philosophical and social beliefs.
  • Empiricism: A theory that states that knowledge comes only or primarily from sensory experience and evidence, emphasizing the role of observation in the formation of ideas.
  • Discourse: In the context of literary theory and social science (notably Michel Foucault), it refers to systems of thoughts composed of ideas, attitudes, courses of action, beliefs, and practices that systematically construct the subjects and the worlds of which they speak.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Which American critic first used the term &#39;New Historicism&#39; in his book &#39;Renaissance Self-Fashioning : From More to Shakespeare&#39; ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Stephen Greenblatt.

Key Points

  • Stephen Greenblatt is a renowned American literary critic and theorist, widely considered the primary founder of the school of literary theory known as New Historicism.
  • He first used the term "New Historicism" in the introduction to his 1980 book, "Renaissance Self-Fashioning: From More to Shakespeare."
  • New Historicism, which Greenblatt sometimes refers to as "cultural poetics," focuses on the relationship between a literary text and the social, cultural, and political context in which it was produced.
  • In "Renaissance Self-Fashioning," Greenblatt explores how 16th-century writers like Thomas More, Edmund Spenser, and William Shakespeare "fashioned" their identities in response to the social and political pressures of the Tudor era.
  • Greenblatt's work was deeply influenced by the theories of Michel Foucault, particularly concerning the way power operates within a society and its discourses.
  • His approach often involves "thick description"—analyzing small, seemingly insignificant historical anecdotes alongside major literary texts to reveal the underlying power structures of a period.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 3.

Additional Information

  • Michel Foucault: A French philosopher whose concepts of power, knowledge, and discourse provided the theoretical underpinnings for New Historicism, although he did not coin the term himself.
  • Edward Said: A Palestinian-American professor and critic who founded the field of post-colonial studies with his landmark work "Orientalism" (1978).
  • Bill Ashcroft: An Australian academic and a key figure in post-colonial theory, best known for co-authoring "The Empire Writes Back" (1989).

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Which of the following concepts is central to Edward Said&#39;s theory of Orientalism ?</span>
options: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The concept of unconscious mind</span> /
<span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The linguistic structure of texts</span> /
<span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The East is identified as the &#39;Other&#39;</span> /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is The East is identified as the 'Other'.

Key Points

  • Edward Said’s seminal work, "Orientalism" (1978), is a foundational text in post-colonial studies that critiques Western representations of the Eastern world.
  • The central concept of the theory is that the West (the Occident) constructs the East (the Orient) as its 'Other' to establish its own identity and superiority.
  • This 'Othering' involves creating binary oppositions where the West is depicted as rational, progressive, and masculine, while the East is portrayed as irrational, stagnant, mysterious, and feminized.
  • Said argues that Orientalism is not just a collection of myths but a discourse—a systematic way of producing knowledge that allowed the West to dominate and exercise authority over the Orient.
  • He suggests that the knowledge produced about the East by scholars, writers, and artists was inextricably linked to the geopolitical power of European imperial structures.
  • The theory highlights how Western academic and artistic descriptions of the "East" often ignored the actual lived realities and diversity of the people living there.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • Michel Foucault: His ideas on "discourse" and "power-knowledge" heavily influenced Said’s methodology in analyzing how the West "knows" and controls the East.
  • Homi K. Bhabha: A post-colonial theorist who expanded on these ideas through concepts like hybridity, mimicry, and ambivalence in colonial relationships.
  • Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak: Known for her essay "Can the Subaltern Speak?", she critiqued the way Western discourse often silences the voices of the oppressed or marginalized ("the subaltern").
  • Binary Opposition: A structuralist concept that Said uses to show how Western identity depends on the exclusion or degradation of an opposite (the East).

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">According to Peter Barry&#39;s book &#39;Beginning Theory&#39;, what does the term &#39;Adept Phase&#39; mean in post-colonial criticism ?</span>
options: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The writers and critics follow the European form and norms to describe their experience.</span> /
<span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The writers modify the forms and norms to their own specifications.</span> /
<span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The colonial mind becomes independent, creative and really &#39;cross-cultural&#39; in expressing its experience.</span> /
<span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">The writers write only about the Feminist experiences.</span> /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is The colonial mind becomes independent, creative and really 'cross-cultural' in expressing its experience.

Key Points

  • In his influential book 'Beginning Theory: An Introduction to Literary and Cultural Theory', Peter Barry outlines the three phases of post-colonial literature: Adopt, Adapt, and Adept.
  • The 'Adept Phase' is the final stage of development where the post-colonial writer achieves complete intellectual and creative independence.
  • In this phase, the writer no longer looks to the European "center" for validation or as a primary model to imitate or modify.
  • The colonial mind becomes truly "cross-cultural," creating new forms that express colonial experience with confidence and artistic maturity.
  • This phase represents the decoupling of literature from the colonial power's standards, allowing for a unique voice that is neither mimicry nor simple adaptation.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 3.

Additional Information

  • Adopt Phase: The first phase where writers follow European forms and norms. They accept the validity of the European model and attempt to write as if they were part of that tradition (imitation).
  • Adapt Phase: The middle phase where writers begin to adapt European forms to their own subject matter. They might use a traditional European genre (like the novel) but fill it with local characters, settings, and linguistic nuances.
  • Peter Barry: A British academic and critic whose book "Beginning Theory" (first published in 1995) remains one of the most popular introductory texts for students studying literary theory.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Which of the following influential theoreticians in Marxist criticism was a French Marxist thinker ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Louis Althusser.

Key Points

  • Louis Althusser (1918–1990) was a prominent French Marxist philosopher who significantly influenced Marxist criticism and structuralist thought.
  • He was a long-time member of the French Communist Party and taught at the École Normale Supérieure in Paris.
  • Althusser is best known for his work in structural Marxism, which sought to analyze Marxism as a rigorous science rather than a humanistic philosophy.
  • One of his most influential contributions is the concept of Ideological State Apparatuses (ISAs), which explains how the state maintains control through institutions like schools, churches, and the media rather than just through physical force.
  • He introduced the term interpellation, describing the process by which individuals are recruited into ideologies and constituted as subjects.
  • His major works include For Marx (1965) and Reading Capital (1965), which had a profound impact on 20th-century critical theory.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • Frederic Jameson: An influential American literary critic and Marxist political theorist, renowned for his analysis of postmodernism and the political unconscious.
  • Terry Eagleton: A prominent British literary theorist and critic known for his widely read introductions to literary theory and his Marxist interpretations of literature.
  • Antonio Gramsci: An Italian Marxist philosopher and politician, famous for developing the theory of cultural hegemony, which explores how the ruling class maintains power through cultural means.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Which one of the following is a primary source in literary research ?</span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Works by the Author.

Key Points

  • In literary research, a primary source is the direct object of study—the original creative work or document produced by the author or during the time period under investigation.
  • Works by the Author (such as novels, poems, plays, short stories, and essays) are considered primary sources because they provide the firsthand evidence and raw material for analysis.
  • Primary sources also include original letters, diaries, manuscripts, and autobiographies written by the author themselves.
  • These sources are essential for literary scholars to form their own interpretations and arguments before consulting external evaluations.
  • Distinguishing between primary and secondary sources is a fundamental step in the research process to ensure the validity and depth of literary analysis.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 3.

Additional Information

  • Literary Criticism: This is a secondary source. It involves the interpretation, analysis, and evaluation of primary literary works by other scholars.
  • Author's Biography: This is generally a secondary source as it is an account of the author's life written by someone else, often synthesizing various primary and secondary materials.
  • Journal Articles: These are secondary sources (or tertiary sources) that contain scholarly discussions, peer-reviewed research, and critiques regarding authors and their works.

The correct answer is (a), (b), (c) and (d).

Key Points

  • Literary research involves a systematic investigation into texts, their contexts, and their meanings, requiring a variety of tools to organize and analyze data.
  • Bibliography: This is an essential tool for identifying, locating, and listing primary and secondary sources. It ensures the researcher has a comprehensive overview of existing scholarship on a topic.
  • Note System: Research involves gathering vast amounts of information. A note system (such as the traditional card index system or modern digital tools like Zotero or Evernote) is used to systematically record and organize quotes, ideas, and references.
  • Scientific Tools: In the context of literary research, these refer to rigorous, systematic methods and technological aids. This includes digital humanities tools like concordances, stylometric software for authorship attribution, and forensic tools for analyzing physical manuscripts (paper, ink, and binding).
  • Textual Analysis: This is the fundamental methodology of literary research. It involves the close examination of a text's language, structure, and themes to derive meaning and interpretation.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • MLA Style: The Modern Language Association provides the standard guidelines for bibliography and formatting in literary research.
  • Primary Sources: These are original materials such as novels, poems, plays, letters, and manuscripts that are the direct object of study.
  • Secondary Sources: These consist of critiques, biographies, and historical studies that provide analysis and commentary on primary sources.
  • Digital Humanities: An emerging field that specifically uses "scientific" computing tools to analyze large corpora of literary texts (distant reading).

questions.statement: When citing a journal article in the MLA format, which of the following is the correct order ?
options: Author(s). "Title of Article". Title of Journal, Volume, Issue, Year, Pages. /
Author(s), Title of Journal, "Title of Article", Volume, Issue, Year, Pages. /
"Title of Article". Author(s), Title of Journal, Volume, Issue, Year, Pages. /
Title of Journal, Author(s), "Title of Article", Volume, Issue, Year, Pages. /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Author(s). "Title of Article". Title of Journal, Volume, Issue, Year, Pages.

Key Points

  • The Modern Language Association (MLA) style is the standard formatting style used in the humanities, particularly in writing about language and literature.
  • According to the MLA 8th and 9th editions, the citation of a journal article follows a specific "container" system to ensure consistency.
  • The correct sequence begins with the Author(s), usually listed as Last Name, First Name.
  • The "Title of the Article" follows next and must be enclosed in double quotation marks.
  • The Title of the Journal follows the article title and must be written in italics.
  • After the journal title, the specific publication details are listed: the Volume (preceded by "vol."), the Issue (preceded by "no."), the Year of publication, and the Page range (preceded by "pp.").

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Punctuation: In MLA style, a period follows the Author and the Article Title. Commas are used to separate the Journal Title, Volume, Issue, Year, and Pages.
  • Online Journals: If the article is accessed online, the citation should also include the DOI (Digital Object Identifier) or a URL at the end.
  • In-text Citations: For MLA, in-text citations typically include the author's last name and the page number (e.g., Fanon 45) without a comma between them.

questions.statement: <span style="color: rgb(33, 37, 41);">Which of the following statements are correct ?<br>(a) DOI is a unique alphanumeric string assigned to identify content online.<br>(b) DOI is a random string of numbers assigned to any web page.<br>(c) DOI provides a persistent link to its location on the internet.<br>(d) DOI is an identifier used only for identifying books in a library.<br><strong>Codes :</strong></span>
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Only (a) and (c).

Key Points

  • DOI stands for Digital Object Identifier.
  • It is a unique alphanumeric string assigned by a registration agency (such as the International DOI Foundation) to identify content and provide a persistent link to its location on the internet.
  • Statement (a) is correct because a DOI is indeed a unique string used to identify digital objects like journal articles, datasets, and reports.
  • Statement (c) is correct because the primary purpose of a DOI is to provide a persistent link; even if the URL of the content changes, the DOI remains the same and redirects to the new location.
  • Statement (b) is incorrect because DOIs are not random strings for "any" web page; they are structured identifiers assigned to specific digital objects through formal registration processes.
  • Statement (d) is incorrect because DOIs are not limited to books in a library; they are used across various digital media, and libraries primarily use ISBNs for books.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • ISBN (International Standard Book Number): A unique numeric commercial book identifier used primarily for books.
  • ISSN (International Standard Serial Number): An eight-digit serial number used to uniquely identify a serial publication, such as a magazine or journal.
  • URL (Uniform Resource Locator): A web address that specifies the location of a resource on the internet, which can change over time (unlike the persistent DOI).
  • ORCID: A non-proprietary alphanumeric code to uniquely identify scientific and other academic authors and contributors.

questions.statement: Which one of the following statements is correct following MLA format ?
options: The first line of each paragraph should be indented one inch from the left margin. /
Indentation is not required in MLA format. /
The first line of each paragraph should be indented half an inch from the left margin. /
The first line of each paragraph should be indented one and half an inch from the left margin. /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is The first line of each paragraph should be indented half an inch from the left margin.

Key Points

  • MLA Style (Modern Language Association) is a set of guidelines for formatting papers and citing sources, commonly used in the humanities.
  • According to MLA formatting rules, the first line of every paragraph must be indented exactly half an inch (0.5") from the left margin.
  • This indentation is usually achieved by pressing the "Tab" key once on a standard keyboard.
  • The purpose of this indentation is to provide a clear visual cue for the reader to identify the start of a new paragraph.
  • All other lines in the paragraph should remain flush with the left margin (1-inch margin).

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 3.

Additional Information

  • Margins: In MLA format, the margins for the top, bottom, left, and right sides should be 1 inch.
  • Spacing: The entire document, including the Works Cited page and block quotations, should be double-spaced.
  • Font: While MLA does not mandate a specific font, it recommends a legible font (e.g., Times New Roman) in 12-point size.
  • Hanging Indents: While paragraphs use a first-line indent, the Works Cited page uses "hanging indents," where the second and subsequent lines of an entry are indented half an inch.
  • Block Quotations: If a quotation is longer than four lines of prose, it is formatted as a block, indented 0.5 inches from the left margin entirely.

questions.statement:
Given below are two statements :
(1) Author’s interviews is a primary source.
(2) Literary analyses and criticisms are secondary sources.
Choose the appropriate option :
options: Both statements (1) and (2) are correct /
Both statements (1) and (2) are incorrect /
Statement (1) is correct and statement (2) is incorrect /
Statement (1) is incorrect and statement (2) is correct /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Both statements (1) and (2) are correct.

Key Points

  • Statement (1) is correct: A primary source provides direct or firsthand evidence about an object, person, or event. Since an author's interview contains the creator's own words, thoughts, and reflections on their work or life, it is considered a primary source.
  • Statement (2) is correct: Secondary sources describe, discuss, interpret, comment upon, analyze, evaluate, or summarize primary sources. Literary analyses and criticisms examine the original creative works (the primary sources) and offer interpretations; therefore, they are classified as secondary sources.
  • In the field of literary research, distinguishing between primary and secondary sources is essential for establishing the credibility and depth of an academic argument.
  • Primary sources in literature also include the original novels, poems, plays, diaries, and letters written by the authors themselves.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Primary Sources: Other examples include autobiographies, original photographs, speeches, and raw data from experiments.
  • Secondary Sources: Other examples include biographies, textbooks, magazine articles that interpret findings, and encyclopedias.
  • Tertiary Sources: These are sources that index, abstract, organize, or compile other sources, such as bibliographies, library catalogs, and directories.

questions.statement: Which of the following should not be included in the ‘Works Cited’ page ?
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Personal Communications.

Key Points

  • A 'Works Cited' page (commonly used in MLA style) or a 'Reference' list (used in APA style) is designed to provide readers with the information necessary to locate and retrieve any source cited in the body of a paper.
  • Personal Communications include things like private letters, emails, personal interviews, and telephone conversations.
  • In many academic citation styles, such as APA, personal communications are not included in the final reference list because they do not provide recoverable data for the reader. They are cited in the text only.
  • The fundamental rule of a bibliography or Works Cited page is that it should only contain sources that a reader can access independently.
  • While MLA style does allow for the inclusion of personal interviews in a Works Cited list, in a general research methodology context, personal communications are the category most frequently excluded from formal publication lists due to their non-retrievable nature.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Additional Information

  • Books: These are standard retrievable sources that must always be included in a Works Cited page, including the author, title, publisher, and year of publication.
  • Journal Articles: These are critical scholarly sources found in academic journals and must be included with details like volume, issue number, and page range.
  • Websites: As digital sources, these are included in modern citation lists to guide readers to the online location (URL or DOI) where the information was found.

questions.statement: APA Style, MLA Style, Chicago Manual, OSCOLA are famously known as :
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Citation Manuals.

Key Points

  • APA Style, MLA Style, Chicago Manual, and OSCOLA are sets of rules and standards established for academic writing and the documentation of sources.
  • These are collectively known as Citation Manuals or Style Guides, which help writers avoid plagiarism by providing clear methods for attributing ideas to original authors.
  • APA Style (American Psychological Association): Predominantly used in the social sciences, education, and psychology.
  • MLA Style (Modern Language Association): Commonly used in the humanities, especially in language and literature studies.
  • Chicago Manual of Style (CMOS): Widely used in history, business, and the arts; it is known for its two systems: Notes/Bibliography and Author/Date.
  • OSCOLA (Oxford University Standard for the Citation of Legal Authorities): A specialized style guide used primarily for citing legal materials in the United Kingdom.
  • These manuals provide consistency in formatting, such as margins, font, and the layout of the reference list or bibliography.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Directories: These are systematic lists of names, addresses, or data of individuals, organizations, or files (e.g., a telephone directory or a business directory).
  • Abbreviation Manuals: These are reference works that list shortened forms of words or phrases and their full meanings, often used in technical or specialized fields.
  • Handbooks: A handbook is a type of reference work or other collection of instructions, intended to provide ready reference on a specific subject or field.

questions.statement: Plagiarism detection software checks plagiarism by providing.................. .
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Similarity Index.

Key Points

  • Plagiarism detection software (such as Turnitin, Urkund/Ouriginal, or iThenticate) functions by comparing a submitted document against a massive database of existing content.
  • This database typically includes millions of web pages, archived internet content, academic journals, books, and previously submitted student papers.
  • The primary output of this software is a Similarity Index, which is a percentage indicating the amount of text in the document that matches other sources.
  • A "Similarity Report" highlights the specific passages that match existing works and provides links to the original sources.
  • It is important to understand that a similarity index does not automatically prove plagiarism; it simply identifies matching text. A human reviewer must determine if the matching text is properly cited or if it constitutes plagiarism.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Citation Index: This is a bibliographic database that tracks which documents cite which other documents (e.g., Web of Science, Scopus, or Google Scholar). It is used to measure the impact and reach of scholarly work.
  • H-Index: An author-level metric that attempts to measure both the productivity and citation impact of the publications of a scientist or scholar.
  • Self-Plagiarism: This occurs when an author reuses significant portions of their own previously published work without proper attribution or citation.
  • Peer Review: A process of evaluation involving qualified individuals within the relevant field to ensure the quality and credibility of academic work before publication.

questions.statement: How should you provide in-text citation for a short quotation if author’s name is mentioned in the sentence ?
options: Only the page number should be provided in parenthesis. /
Both the author’s name and page number should be included in parenthesis. /
Name of the books and the page number should be provided in parenthesis. /
No parenthetical citation is required. /
questions.explanation:
The correct answer is Only the page number should be provided in parenthesis.

Key Points

  • According to MLA (Modern Language Association) style, which is commonly used in the humanities, if the author's name is already mentioned in the sentence (often called a signal phrase), it should not be repeated in the parenthetical citation.
  • In such cases, only the page number from which the quotation or paraphrase is taken should be placed in the parentheses at the end of the sentence.
  • For example: Wordsworth stated that Romantic poetry was marked by a "spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings" (263).
  • The parenthetical citation should be placed at the end of the sentence, before the final punctuation mark (period).
  • This method ensures clarity while avoiding redundancy in the text, allowing the reader to easily locate the source in the "Works Cited" list.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

Additional Information

  • Author's name not in sentence: If the author's name is not mentioned in the sentence, both the author's last name and the page number must be included in the parentheses (e.g., (Wordsworth 263)).
  • APA Style: In APA (American Psychological Association) style, the citation includes the author's name and the year of publication (e.g., (Wordsworth, 1800)). If quoting, the page number is also included (e.g., (Wordsworth, 1800, p. 263)).
  • Multiple works: If you are citing multiple works by the same author, a shortened version of the book title is often included in the citation to distinguish between the sources.
